title: FC Drobeta-Turnu Severin

infobox.value: was a [Romanian](/wiki/Romania "Romania") professional [football](/wiki/Football_(soccer) "Football (soccer)") [club](/wiki/Football_team "Football team") from [Drobeta-Turnu Severin](/wiki/Drobeta-Turnu_Severin "Drobeta-Turnu Severin"), [Mehedinți County](/wiki/Mehedin%C8%9Bi_County "Mehedinți County"), founded in 1958 and dissolved in 2011.

**Fotbal Club Drobeta-Turnu Severin** was a [Romanian](/wiki/Romania "Romania") professional [football](/wiki/Football_(soccer) "Football (soccer)") [club](/wiki/Football_team "Football team") from [Drobeta-Turnu Severin](/wiki/Drobeta-Turnu_Severin "Drobeta-Turnu Severin"), [Mehedinți County](/wiki/Mehedin%C8%9Bi_County "Mehedinți County"), founded in 1958 and dissolved in 2011.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Drobeta-Turnu_Severin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The club was founded in 1958 under the name *Drubeta Turnu Severin*, with *Drubeta* being a historical spelling variant of Drobeta, inspired by the ancient [Roman castrum](/wiki/Drobeta_(castra) "Drobeta (castra)") on whose site the town of [Turnu Severin](/wiki/Turnu_Severin "Turnu Severin") stands.[[1]](#cite_note-name-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In its [first season](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_Divizia_C "1958–59 Divizia C"), after taking over [CFR Turnu Severin](/wiki/CFR_Turnu_Severin "CFR Turnu Severin")’s place in [Divizia C](/wiki/Divizia_C "Divizia C"), Drubeta made an immediate impact, finishing 2nd in Series VI and earning promotion to the second division after the competition was disbanded and [Divizia B](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B") was expanded to three series of 14 teams each. In the second division, Drubeta ranked 12th in the [1959–60](/wiki/1959%E2%80%9360_Divizia_B "1959–60 Divizia B") season and 14th in the [following season](/wiki/1960%E2%80%9361_Divizia_B "1960–61 Divizia B"), after which it was relegated to the [regional championship](/wiki/Liga_IV "Liga IV"), as Divizia C was being dissolved once again.[[3]](#cite_note-L2-3)

In the [1961–62](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_Regional_Championship#Oltenia_Region "1961–62 Regional Championship") season, Drubeta went on to win the [Oltenia Regional Championship](/wiki/Liga_IV "Liga IV"), qualifying for the promotion play-offs, where it finished 2nd in Series II held at Piatra Neamț, thereby quickly returning to the second division.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

After this promotion, the club changed its name to Metalul Turnu Severin. However, after [only one season](/wiki/1962%E2%80%9363_Divizia_B "1962–63 Divizia B"), it was relegated to the re-established Divizia C, having finished last in its series.[[3]](#cite_note-L2-3)

In Divizia C, Metalul competed in the West Series. In the [1963–64](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_Divizia_C "1963–64 Divizia C") season, it finished as runners-up and reached the Round of 16 of the Cupa României, where it was defeated 0–3 by Crișul Oradea, followed by a 4th place in [1964–65](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_Divizia_C "1964–65 Divizia C"), 7th in [1965–66](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_Divizia_C "1965–66 Divizia C"), and 12th in the [1966–67](/wiki/1966%E2%80%9367_Divizia_C "1966–67 Divizia C") season.[[6]](#cite_note-L3-6)

In the [1967–68](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_Divizia_C "1967–68 Divizia C") season, Metalul, led by Constantin Lepădatu, finished as runners-up, one point behind [Electroputere Craiova](/wiki/FC_Electroputere_Craiova "FC Electroputere Craiova"), and qualified for the Divizia B promotion/relegation play-offs, where it was ranked 3rd in Group II, held in Arad, thus returning to the [second division](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B"). The squad included Mîniosu, Corcovan, Chițulescu, Budănescu, Constantin, Cîntar, Cîrtog, Șandru, Diaconu, Hîrșova, Prodan, Căprioru, Jimboreanu, Chirițescu, Pelea, Gall, Sava, Floca, and Zaharia.[[7]](#cite_note-fotbal_a-z-7)[[6]](#cite_note-L3-6)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In Divizia B, Metalul competed in Series II, finished 9th in the [1968–69](/wiki/1968%E2%80%9369_Divizia_B "1968–69 Divizia B") season, and was relegated in the [following campaign](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_Divizia_B "1969–70 Divizia B") after being ranked last, a season during which it also reached the Round of 16 of the [Cupa României](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_Divizia_B "1969–70 Divizia B"), eliminating first-division side [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") with a 5–2 victory, before losing 1–3 to another top-flight team, [Argeș Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești").[[3]](#cite_note-L2-3)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In Divizia C, Metalul competed in Series V, finishing 8th in the [1970–71](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_Divizia_C "1970–71 Divizia C") season, and went on to win Series VII in the [1971–72](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_Divizia_C "1971–72 Divizia C") season under the leadership of Constantin Lepădatu, qualifying for the promotion play-off, where it finished 1st in Group III, played in Râmnicu Vâlcea against [Independența Sibiu](/wiki/FC_Inter_Sibiu "FC Inter Sibiu") and [Vagonul Arad](/wiki/Vagonul_Arad "Vagonul Arad"). The squad included Angelescu, Nae, Cîntar, Muschici, Trașcu, Cîrtog, Vișan, Davidescu, Sandu, Jimboreanu, Iacob, Olteanu, Biță, Țîră, Getoiu, Căprioru, and Coculescu.[[7]](#cite_note-fotbal_a-z-7)[[3]](#cite_note-L2-3)[[6]](#cite_note-L3-6)

In 1972, the name of the ancient [Drobeta](/wiki/Drobeta_(castra) "Drobeta (castra)") was added to the city's name, and the club was renamed Metalul Drobeta-Turnu Severin. However, the presence in the second division was again short-lived. It ranked 12th in the [1972–73](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_Divizia_B "1972–73 Divizia B") season under Constantin Lepădatu, and under Constantin Matache, Metalul finished 5th in the [1973–74](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9374_Divizia_B "1973–74 Divizia B") season and 16th at the end of the [1974–75](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Divizia_B "1974–75 Divizia B") season, being relegated back to the third division. In [Divizia C](/wiki/Divizia_C "Divizia C"), it finished 5th in Series VII in the [1975–76](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_Divizia_C "1975–76 Divizia C") season and reached the Round of 32 in the [Cupa României](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "1975–76 Cupa României") before losing to FIL Orăștie 0–0 and 3–4 on penalties.[[3]](#cite_note-L2-3)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1976, it merged with fellow city side MEVA – the team of *Întreprinderea de Materiale pentru Exploatarea Vagoanelor* (lit. 'The Enterprise for Materials for the Exploitation of Railway Wagons') – and formed CSM Drobeta-Turnu Severin (Club Sportiv Muncitoresc, “Workers’ Sports Club”), which took 2nd place in Series VII in the [1976–77](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_Divizia_C "1976–77 Divizia C") season and 1st in the [1977–78](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_Divizia_C "1977–78 Divizia C") in the same series, with Gheorghe Nuțescu on the bench, returning to the second division. However, it relegated after [one season](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_Divizia_B "1978–79 Divizia B") in [Divizia B](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B") after finishing 15th in Series II.[[6]](#cite_note-L3-6)[[3]](#cite_note-L2-3)

Two more seasons in Divizia C followed, finishing 5th in Series VII in [1979–80](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_Divizia_C "1979–80 Divizia C") and returning once again to Divizia B by winning Series VII in the [1980–81](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Divizia_C "1980–81 Divizia C") season, followed by another relegation after two seasons in the second division, in which it was ranked 13th in Series III in [1981–82](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Divizia_B "1981–82 Divizia B") and 15th in Series II in [1982–83](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_Divizia_B "1982–83 Divizia B"). However, the team returned to the second division after [one season](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_Divizia_C "1983–84 Divizia C"), finishing 1st in Series VII under Marin Bojin’s leadership as player-coach, with a core squad consisting of Dulică, Niță, Dumitrășcuță, Urziceanu, Stan, Marcoci, Bilan, Olteanu, Piticu, Al. Cațan, I. Cațan, Litră, Hornoiu, [Țîră](/wiki/F%C4%83nel_%C8%9A%C3%AEr%C4%83 "Fănel Țîră"), Iordache, Mișu, [Tița](/wiki/Valeriu_Ti%C8%9Ba "Valeriu Tița"), and Arnăutu.[[6]](#cite_note-L3-6)[[7]](#cite_note-fotbal_a-z-7)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In Divizia B, Drobeta managed to finish above the relegation line in Series II, ranking 12th in [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Divizia_B "1998–99 Divizia B") under [Nicolae Ungureanu](/wiki/Nicolae_Ungureanu "Nicolae Ungureanu") and, from the start of the second half, Florin Cioroianu, and 11th in [1999–2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Divizia_B "1999–2000 Divizia B"), with Cioroianu replaced during the first half by Gheorghe Cazacu and Marin Bojin taking over in the second half, before being relegated at the end of the [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Divizia_B "2000–01 Divizia B") season after finishing 13th. In the [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Divizia_B "2001–02 Divizia B") season, it competed in Series V of Divizia C but withdrew from the championship during the winter break.[[3]](#cite_note-L2-3)

In 2007, Severnav Drobeta-Turnu Severin was promoted to [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") and changed its name to FC Drobeta-Turnu Severin, thus reviving the traditional club from Drobeta-Turnu Severin. In the [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Liga_II "2007–08 Liga II") season, the team finished 5th in Series II and reached the Round of 32 of the [Cupa României](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2007–08 Cupa României"), where it lost 1–3 to [Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț](/wiki/Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Liga_II "2008–09 Liga II") season, [Iulian Mihăescu](/wiki/Iulian_Mih%C4%83escu "Iulian Mihăescu") was appointed head coach but left during the winter break while the team was 2nd, three points behind the Series II leader, to become [Ioan Andone](/wiki/Ioan_Andone "Ioan Andone")’s assistant at [Al Ettifaq](/wiki/Al_Ettifaq "Al Ettifaq").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) He was replaced by [Eugen Neagoe](/wiki/Eugen_Neagoe "Eugen Neagoe"), who lasted only four matches, after which club president Vasile Mănescu took over and led Drobeta to a 5th-place finish.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Marian Bucurescu returned for the [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Liga_II "2009–10 Liga II") campaign but resigned after four matches, being replaced by [Valeriu Tița](/wiki/Valeriu_Ti%C8%9Ba "Valeriu Tița").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) In February 2010, FC Drobeta-Turnu Severin withdrew after its investors withdrew their financial support. As a result, the club lost all remaining matches by 0–3 forfeit. This marked the club’s second dissolution, after the first one in 2002.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In August 2010, FC Drobeta-Turnu Severin was refounded by the local municipality and enrolled in the [2010–11 Liga III](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Liga_III "2010–11 Liga III") Liga III.[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Cornel Mihart](/wiki/Cornel_Mihart "Cornel Mihart") was appointed head coach, but only until the third round, when [Viorel Ion](/wiki/Viorel_Ion "Viorel Ion") took over, with Mihart remaining as assistant. Viorel Ion lasted ten matchdays, after which he was replaced by Cornel Mihart.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Following an FRF decision denying the club promotion rights to Liga II for three seasons, the municipality withdrew its support, and FC Drobeta-Turnu Severin was transferred to the [Calafat](/wiki/Calafat "Calafat") Municipality, playing its home matches at Dunărea Stadium under head coach Claudiu Oncică. However, in April 2011, the club was dissolved for the third time in its history due to financial problems.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Drobeta-Turnu_Severin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Honours")]

**[Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II")**

* Runners-up (1): [1989–90](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_Divizia_B "1989–90 Divizia B")

**[Liga III](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III")**

* **Winners (4):** [1971–72](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_Divizia_C "1971–72 Divizia C"), [1977–78](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_Divizia_C "1977–78 Divizia C"), [1980–81](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Divizia_C "1980–81 Divizia C"), [1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_Divizia_C "1983–84 Divizia C")
* Runners-up (7): [1958–59](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_Divizia_C "1958–59 Divizia C"), [1963–64](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_Divizia_C "1963–64 Divizia C"), [1967–68](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_Divizia_C "1967–68 Divizia C"), [1976–77](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_Divizia_C "1976–77 Divizia C"), [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Divizia_C "1995–96 Divizia C"), [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Divizia_C "1996–97 Divizia C"), [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Divizia_C "1997–98 Divizia C")
